bio.bio_blurb: Development engineer of the Isolette incubator

career.notes: production of the Isolette infant incubator and oxygen-related equipment

narrative_blocks.header: A chunk of the Nautilus on his desk
narrative_blocks.body: The development engineer behind Isolette incubator and oxygen-equipment production kept a launch souvenir of the world’s first nuclear submarine: twin bronze medals set in heavy hull steel from Electric Boat, where the Nautilus went down the ways in 1954.

narrative_blocks.header: First in French, far from it in drawing
narrative_blocks.body: At West Point he stood first in French and Spanish “without having to work very hard” and was far from distinguished in drawing, his classmate Dorst remembered. Five frontier years with the 4th Cavalry followed — scouting from Fort Clark’s armed camps, where no one walked two hundred yards unarmed.

A volunteer on Crook’s Powder River Expedition after the Custer disaster, he rode in Mackenzie’s November 1876 destruction of the Cheyenne village — and left on sick leave, his eyes burned by glare off the snow. He resigned in 1878, “regretted, loved and respected.”

narrative_blocks.header: Seven thousand acres of American tea
narrative_blocks.body: Commanding the 3rd Connecticut Volunteers in South Carolina in 1898, the colonel saw commercial promise in southern tea and bought four Colleton County plantations — Holly Grove, Rutledge Island, Mowbray, Turkey Hill — whose flourishing gardens and factory his son Frederick managed.

narrative_blocks.header: The Russian ambassador took his house
narrative_blocks.body: The stately residence he built at 1634 I Street, facing Farragut Square in Washington, later housed the Russian ambassador. He was president of New London’s Pequot Casino Association at his death, a clubman to the last afternoon.

narrative_blocks.header: Born on his brother’s thirteenth birthday
narrative_blocks.body: May 2, 1851 — thirteen years to the day after his brother Edmund, born May 2, 1838. The Tyler brothers shared a birthday a generation apart.

cross_connections.display_label: was the patent-medicine magnate father of his wife Cornelia
fought at Powder River in March; he rode there in November

bio.bio_blurb: Cavalry officer turned American tea planter

military_service.notes: Texas frontier 1873-78; volunteer on Crook’s Powder River Expedition, Nov-Dec 1876; resigned July 1, 1878
Camps Haven, Meade, and Marion; honorably discharged Jan 31, 1899

career.organization: American Tea Growing Co., Colleton County SC

bio.bio_blurb: First woman to write Broadway comic opera

narrative_blocks.header: The first woman to write Broadway comic opera
narrative_blocks.body: Her A Venetian Romance at the Knickerbocker Theater made her the first woman to author a comic opera for a Broadway hearing — though she was “too modest to allow her photographs to be used for the public prints.” Richard Mansfield, a frequent houseguest, pushed the piece to production.

narrative_blocks.header: The dancing-master’s son at her piano
narrative_blocks.body: The boy pianist hired for her daughters’ dance party turned out to be the son of the man who had taught her dancing as a girl in Norwich. She installed Frederic Coit Wright as household musician, paid for his training, and wrote the opera with him.

narrative_blocks.header: Lady Bountiful of the Pequot colony
narrative_blocks.body: Her summer home, The Elm, anchored New London’s aristocratic Pequot colony with the most splendid entertainments; the town called her Lady Bountiful for her charity. Yet she refused to let a single photograph reach the public prints.

orbit_connections.name: Theodore Roosevelt (X00744) — married great-granddaughter of Daniel Tyler's parents
orbit_connections.relationship: Through his parents Sarah Edwards Tyler (H00936) and Capt. Daniel Tyler, Brig. Gen. Tyler is an ancestor of Edith Kermit Carow (1861-1948), who became Theodore Roosevelt's second wife and First Lady.

notable.notable_blurb: Union Civil War general and ironmaster

cross_connections.display_label: built an iron empire in Gilded Age Los Angeles
was his nephew and rose to Union general too

bio.bio_blurb: Union general, ironmaster, founder of Anniston

military_service.unit: 1st Connecticut Infantry; division, Army of Northeastern Virginia
military_service.notes: Commanded a division at First Bull Run; led a brigade at the Siege of Corinth; briefly commanded Camp Douglas.

narrative_blocks.header: Blamed for the Union rout at Bull Run
narrative_blocks.body: Tyler commanded a division at the First Battle of Bull Run in 1861 and absorbed much of the blame for the Union defeat, mustered out within weeks. Recommissioned in 1862, he led troops at the siege of Corinth and briefly ran the Camp Douglas prison camp.

narrative_blocks.header: He founded the Alabama city of Anniston
narrative_blocks.body: After resigning from the army in 1864, Tyler built blast furnaces and ran railroads, finding far more success in rails than in pig iron. In the 1870s he established the Woodstock Iron Company and helped found Anniston, named for his daughter-in-law Annie.

narrative_blocks.header: West Point artillerist who studied in France
narrative_blocks.body: Graduating from West Point in 1819, Tyler became an authority on artillery, studying at the French school at Metz and translating French gunnery manuals into English. His honesty inspecting private arms contractors may have stalled his promotion beyond first lieutenant.

bio.bio_blurb: Patent-medicine magnate; Norwich Free Academy founder

notable.notable_blurb: Patent-medicine magnate; Norwich Free Academy founder

career.location: Providence RI, then Monroe, Michigan Territory

career.organization: Charles Osgood & Co. wholesale drugs, Norwich

narrative_blocks.header: A patent medicine built the fortune
narrative_blocks.body: “Dr. Osgood’s India Cholagogue,” his remedy for fever and ague formulated on the Michigan frontier, sold in impressive quantities for fifty years — funding the Norwich drug house, the Shetucket Bank, a railroad presidency, and a founder’s share of the Norwich Free Academy.

narrative_blocks.header: Five hundred stampless letters survive him
narrative_blocks.body: Grand Valley State University holds more than nine hundred of his papers — including over five hundred stampless folded letters from his Michigan land-speculation years, among them seven bearing the six-month-lived Havre post office’s cancellations. Philatelists prize the collection.

Ill health forced him from the mayoralty in 1876 — his cousin Hugh Henry Osgood held the office before him and resumed it after, and his son Frederic took it in 1896. The India Cholagogue fortune stood behind them all.

burialCemetery.name: Cedar Grove Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: New London, New London County CT. FGA #103253. Contains H02695 Martha Anne Strong Harris and I01220 Jonathan Newton Harris.
